coredata.dc:title: An electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for analysis of bisphenol A based on carbon nanodots composite as co-reaction of Ru(bpy)3 2+ nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiaohong
Luo, Lijun
Li, Libo
Di, Zhanxuan
Zhang, Jiayi
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: An electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor based on poly(ethylenimine) functionalized nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCDs@PEI) composite as co-reactant, and Ru(bpy)3 2+ nanosheets (RuNSs) as luminophor was constructed for the specific analysis of bisphenol A (BPA). On account of the double improvement functions of NCDs and PEI on RuNSs, the as-developed aptasensor exhibited superior ECL properties. In order to further enhance the selectivity of the RuNSs-NCDs@PEI system for BPA detection, we innovatively introduced the BPA aptamer to construct a novel ECL aptasensor. Compared with that without aptamer and with random DNA, the constructed aptasensor performed higher specificity for BPA determination under the interference of structural analogues. The as-prepared ECL aptasensor exhibited a wide linear range of 1.0 × 10−10 - 1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1, and a relatively low detection limit of 3.3 × 10−11 mol L−1 for BPA detection with good stability and repeatability. Finally, when the ECL aptasensor made use of BPA analysis in water samples, the obtained results were confirmed by high performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet spectrum (HPLC-UV) method, verifying its good accuracy. The novel aptasensor provided a new sensing platform for BPA in water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ru(bpy)3 2+ nanosheets
poly(ethylenimine) functionalized nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
Electrochemiluminescence aptasensor
Bisphenol A

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterization of the different modified electrodes
Electrochemistry (EC) and ECL behaviors of the modified electrodes
The optimizatLD, fluorescence detection; GCE/AuNPs/rGO-Fc-NH2, AuNPs/reduced graphene oxidion of experimental parameters
Analytical performances of the BPA ECL aptasensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21675065 , 61801195 ), the “ 333 Project ” ( BRA2017390 ) and the Six Categories Talent Peak of Jiangsu Province ( NY-011 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2018M632238 ), the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions ( 2014–37 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) Wide-scan XPS spectrum, (C) FT-IR spectrum and (D) UV-Vis absorption spectrum and FL spectrum of the prepared NCDs@PEI. Inset of (D), the photographs of NCDs@PEI under white and 365 nm UV light, respectively.
SEM images of (A) GCE, (B) RuNSs/GCE, (C) NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE, (D) AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE.
(A) CV and (B) ECL-potential responses of a: bare GCE, b: RuNSs/GCE, c: NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE, d: AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE. Conditions: PBS, 0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.5; Scan rate, 0.1 V s−1.

(A) CV (B) EIS and (C) ECL-potential responses of different modified electrodes: a: AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE, b: Apt/AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE, c: BSA/Apt/AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE, d: BPA/BSA/Apt/AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE. CV and EIS curves were conducted in 5.0 × 10−3 mol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution containing 0.1 mol L−1 KCl. The ECL responses were measured in 0.1 mol L−1 PBS (pH 7.5). (D) The ECL response with (red curve) and without (blue curve) BPA at different modified electrodes without aptamer, with aptamer and with random DNA. Conditions: PBS, 0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.5; Scan rate, 0.1 V s−1. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Optimization of (A) electrodeposition time, (B) electrodeposition potential, (C) concentration of aptamer, (D) incubation time between aptamer and AuPs, (E) binding time of BPA with aptamer and (F) pH value of buffer solution. Conditions: PBS, 0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.5; Scan rate, 0.1 V s−1.

(A) The ECL intensity of aptasensor at different concentrations of BPA. (B) Linear calibration between logarithmic value of BPA concentration and the ECL intensity. Specificity of the sensor in the absence (C) and presence (D) BPA aptamer incubated with different interfering substances. (E) Stability of the BPA aptasensor for 12 successive measurements. (F) Repeatability for BPA detection by 5 parallel measurements. Conditions: PBS, 0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.5; Scan rate, 0.1 V s−1.
Schematic illustration of the ECL aptasensor based on RuNSs-NCDs@PEI system for BPA detection.
Comparison of analytical performances of several methods reported to detect BPA.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Table 1. FLD, fluorescence detection; GCE/AuNPs/rGO-Fc-NH2, AuNPs/reduced graphene oxide (GO)-grafted (4-ferrocenylethyne) phenylamine (Fc-NH2) modified GCE; GCE/PVA/PdNPs-GR/Ru(bpy)3 2+, polyvinyl alcohol/Graphene-palladium nanoparticles/Ru(bpy)3 2+ modified glassy carbon electrode; Au/f-MWCNTs/AuNPs, functionalized multiwall carbon nanotubes/gold nanoparticles nanocomposite film modified gold electrode; CPE/SnSe/GR, SnSe/graphene nanohybrids modified carbon paste electrode, UCNPs-based aptasensor, upconversion nanoparticles-based aptasensor.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of the different concentrations of BPA in tap water and river water using the proposed aptasensor and HPLC-UV methods (n = 3).

head.ce:title: An electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for analysis of bisphenol A based on carbon nanodots composite as co-reaction of Ru(bpy)3 2+ nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Institute of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Institute of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor based on poly(ethylenimine) functionalized nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCDs@PEI) composite as co-reactant, and Ru(bpy)3 2+ nanosheets (RuNSs) as luminophor was constructed for the specific analysis of bisphenol A (BPA). On account of the double improvement functions of NCDs and PEI on RuNSs, the as-developed aptasensor exhibited superior ECL properties. In order to further enhance the selectivity of the RuNSs-NCDs@PEI system for BPA detection, we innovatively introduced the BPA aptamer to construct a novel ECL aptasensor. Compared with that without aptamer and with random DNA, the constructed aptasensor performed higher specificity for BPA determination under the interference of structural analogues. The as-prepared ECL aptasensor exhibited a wide linear range of 1.0 × 10−10 - 1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1, and a relatively low detection limit of 3.3 × 10−11 mol L−1 for BPA detection with good stability and repeatability. Finally, when the ECL aptasensor made use of BPA analysis in water samples, the obtained results were confirmed by high performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet spectrum (HPLC-UV) method, verifying its good accuracy. The novel aptasensor provided a new sensing platform for BPA in water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: As an industrial synthetic organic chemical, bisphenol A (BPA) is proverbially utilized to synthesize polycarbonate plastics and epoxy resins [1,2]. BPA is not only defined as an environmental pollutant, but also an endocrine disrupting chemical [2,3]. Furthermore, excessive BPA can increase incidence of cancers, reduce immune function, decrease fertility capacity and lead to other unknown diseases in human and animals [4–6]. Consequently, a large number of scientists have developed many analytical methods for BPA, such as high performance liquid chromatographic-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [7,8], gas chromatographic-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [9], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [10] and capillary electrophoresis-ultraviolet spectrum (CE-UV) [11], etc. These methods possess high accuracy and reliability, while also have some defects such as complicated sample pretreatment, professional operator or time-consuming process. Recently, some electrochemical methods have been widely used in BPA analysis [12–14]. Nevertheless, the high overpotential of BPA oxidation may cause poor performance at electrode surface, which can further lead to unsatisfactory reproducibility and selectivity [15]. Therefore, developing a new simple and economic method for BPA analysis is still meaningful and important from healthy and environmental viewpoints.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) has been widely applied for BPA detection due to its simple set-up and good sensitivity [16,17]. The most typical ECL system for BPA detection is tris (2, 2-bipyridyl) dichlororuthenium (II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) combined with its co-reactants, mainly tripropylamine (TPA) system [18,19]. However, TPA shows some disadvantages, including toxicity, volatility, slow electrochemical oxidation rate and low sensitivity at low concentration [20]. Therefore, many efforts have been put to develop novel, eco-friendly and effective co-reactants to replace TPA. Currently, carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs), and their functionalized complexes have been successfully adopted as co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ because of their low toxicity, good biocompatibility and superior optical properties [21,22]. It has been shown that the role of CQDs as co-reactant is similar to TPA, and the main functional group is the benzyl alcohol structure on the CQDs surface [21]. We also carried out some related researches of carbon nanodots composites as co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ [23–25]. Results demonstrate that the prepared nano-composite, defined as poly(ethylenimine) functionalized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs@PEI), exhibits an higher ECL enhancement effect for Ru(bpy)3 2+ than that of pure NCDs. Utilizing the established Ru(bpy)3 2+-NCDs@PEI system, a novel ECL-based sensor with good analytical performances was firstly reported [25]. However, the selectivity of the sensing system needs to be improved because of the absence of any specific recognition element.

In order to further improve the selectivity, aptamer can be introduced to construct the aptasensor for BPA detection. However, the current BPA aptasenors are mainly focused on electrochemical and photoelectrochemical methods [26–28]. ECL aptasensors of BPA are rarely reported up to now, and only upconversion nanoparticles and dichlorotris (1, 10-phenanthroline) ruthenium (II) hydrate (Ru(phen)3 2+)-TPA system-based ECL aptasenors are developed [29,30]. To our knowledge, Ru(bpy)3 2+-NCDs@PEI system has not been applied for fabricating the ECL aptasensor for BPA with high sensitivity and specificity so far.

Herein, on the basis of the previous work [25], we firstly reported a novel ECL aptasensor for BPA determination with Ru(bpy)3 2+ nanosheets (RuNSs) as luminophor and NCDs@PEI as co-reactant. The introduction of BPA aptamer into RuNSs-NCDs@PEI ECL system effectively improved the selectivity for BPA detection. Consequently, structure-like interference agents including 4′-dihydroxydiphenyl, hydroquinone, diethylstilbestrol and 3-chlorophenol with 50-fold concentration did not disturb the BPA determination, demonstrating that the constructed aptasensor was of higher specificity. The as-constructed ECL aptasensor provided a wide linear range and a relatively low detection limit for BPA detection. Conclusively, the ECL-based BPA aptasensor was successfully applied in water samples, and this obtained results consisted well with those of HPLC-UV method, illustrating good accuracy of the aptamer sensing platform.

The preparation process of BPA ECL aptasensor was represented in Scheme 1 . Before modification, the conventional process was used to clean the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) [25]. After drying in air, Ru(bpy)3 2+ organic solution (acetonitrile:n-propanol, 5:1, v/v) was placed on the GCE surface to obtain RuNSs modified GCE (RuNSs/GCE) [31]. After that, 3 μL 1.0 mg mL−1 NCDs@PEI- reduced graphene oxide was dropped to get a novel ECL interface (NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE). 3 μL 0.5 wt% Nafion was adopted to fix materials. Next, this modified electrode was electrodeposited at - 0.2 V for 15 s by immersing into 1% HAuCl4 solution to further obtain gold particles (AuPs) modified electrode. Thereafter, 3 μL 2.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 thiol functionalized BPA aptamer (5-SH–(CH2)6–CCG GTG GGT GGT CAG GTG GGA TAG CGT TCC GCG TAT GGC CCA GCG CAT CAC GGG TTC GCA CCA-3′) was continuously immobilized by Au–S covalent bond at 4 °C for 14 h (Apt/AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE). Subsequently, the self-assembly electrode was immersed in 80 μL 1% bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution for specific time at 37 °C. Lastly, the BSA/Apt/AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE was incubated with 500 μL BPA solution for 40 min (25 °C) to capture and detect BPA. In addition, the modified electrode was thoroughly rinsed with the corresponding washing buﬀer for several times after each modification step.
The store solution of BPA (1.0 × 10−2 mol L−1) was prepared in ethanol. The different required concentrations for BPA analysis were diluted by Tris-HCl buffer.

The real samples were collected in water-tap and Yangtze River in Zhenjiang, China, respectively. The obtained samples were treated according to the previous report [32]. Prior to analysis, 0.22 μm cellulose membrane was chosen to filter impurities of samples. After then, the samples were further diluted to acquire the required concentrations.

NCDs@PEI was prepared according to the previous report [25] and the corresponding characterization results are shown in Fig. 1 . From transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image, NCDs@PEI is well-dispersed and the average diameter is 15.4 nm (Fig. 1A). Fig. 1B shows the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of three apparent peaks located at 284.9, 399.4 and 531.4 eV relating to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. As seen from fourier transformed infrared spectra (FT-IR) measurement (Fig. 1C), the peak at 3446 cm−1 exhibits N–H and O–H bonds [33–35]. The peak at 1642 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO in NCDs@PEI [34,35]. The peak appeared at 1417 cm−1 is assigned to C–H stretching vibration deformation [25]. And the peak at 1006 cm−1 is ascribed to C–O stretching vibrations in the oxygen-containing centrality [35]. From UV-visible (UV-Vis) spectrum (Fig. 1D), NCDs@PEI appears a distinct peak centered at 344 nm. Inset of Fig. 1D shows that yellowish-clear NCDs@PEI exhibits strong blue luminescence under 365 nm UV light. In addition, fluorescence (FL) spectra of Fig. 1D demonstrate that the maximum excitation of NCDs@PEI is at 363 nm, while emission wavelength is at 470 nm. All the characterization results of NCDs@PEI agree very well with the previous report [25], demonstrating that NCDs@PEI is successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterization of the different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and microstructure of bare GCE, RuNSs/GCE, NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE, and AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE were investigated by SEM. Bare GCE exhibits a clean interface (Fig. 2 A). Upon the modification of Ru(bpy)3 2+ organic solution, RuNSs with the average size of 1.94 μm are produced [31] (Fig. 2B). After further addition of NCDs@PEI-rGO (Fig. 2C), the surface of RuNSs/GCE is covered with rGO, where the monolayer sheet is similar to that of pure rGO (Fig S1). As expected, NCDs@PEI is too small (15.4 nm) to observe in the SEM image. Herein, rGO is introduced for two purposes: one is that rGO can be used as carrier to improve the dispersibility of NCDs@PEI, and the other is that the conductivity of the ECL system is expected to be increased, which may lead to a higher ECL intensity [25]. Fig. 2D illustrates that AuPs are successfully electrodeposited on the electrode surface. The average diameter of AuPs is 0.6 μm, and the particle size distribution is uniform, which is benefited to fix BPA aptamer through Au–S bond. The above results illustrate that each step of the electrode is successfully modified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemistry (EC) and ECL behaviors of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: EC and its corresponding ECL behaviors were performed by scanning the modified electrodes in PBS buffer. From inset of Fig. 3 A, bare GCE (curve a) shows no redox peaks, while RuNSs/GCE (curve b) gives a pair of apparent redox peaks of Ru(bpy)3 2+ at 1.12 V and 1.04 V, respectively. Upon the addition of NCDs@PEI-rGO (Fig. 3A), oxidation peak current obviously increases (curve c), demonstrating that composite has favourable catalytic properties for RuNSs. After electrodeposition of AuPs (curve d), the oxidation peak current further increases, which is ascribed to the good conductivity of AuPs that increases the electron transfer rate and magnifies the electrochemical signal. Moreover, the reduction peak of AuPs at 0.49 V is observed, illustrating that AuPs are successfully modified [36].

Fig. 3B shows the corresponding ECL responses at different modified electrodes. There is no ECL intensity at GCE (curve a). And the ECL intensity of RuNSs/GCE is relatively weak (90 a.u.) due to the slow charge transfer rate and the absence of co-reactant (curve b). After adding NCDs@PEI-rGO, the ECL signal increases rapidly with 84-fold enhancement (curve c). The enhancement effects should be attributed to two aspects. Firstly, the addition of rGO (curve b) can increase the ECL intensity of RuNSs by 3.7-fold (Fig. S2) because of good conductivity of rGO [25], and NCDs@PEI (curve c) can increase it by 34.2-fold owing to good co-reactant performance of NCDs@PEI. While combining the dual catalytic effects of NCDs@PEI and rGO, NCDs@PEI-rGO results in the highest intensity of 7554.a.u., with 84-fold enhancement in comparison with RuNSs. Secondly, the ECL intensities obtained by adding NCDs@PEI into the buffer solution and modifying NCDs@PEI on the surface of electrode were compared. Fig. S3 illustrates that the simultaneous modification of RuNSs and NCDs@PEI on the sensing interface produces much higher ECL signal (curve c, 3474 a.u.) than that of adding NCDs@PEI into the solution (curve b, 998 a.u.), increasing by 38.6-fold and 10-fold of ECL signal in comparison with RuNSs, respectively. This can be explained that NCDs@PEI in the solution needs response time to diffuse to the surface of the electrode [19,37], which causes lower signal than the simultaneous modification. Therefore, NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE shows a very strong ECL signal strength. Finally, the introduction of AuPs enables the ECL intensity up to 8793 a.u. (Fig. 3B, curve d), due to the good conductivity performance of AuPs, which can improve the electron transfer on the GCE and enhanced the ECL intensity [38].

The changed interface properties of each modified steps were effectively monitored. Fig. 4 A shows the CV behaviors of the ECL aptasensor in KCl with [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- as electrochemical probe. Its redox peaks were observed at 0.29 V and 0.19 V on AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE. Upon the addition of BPA aptamer, the redox current obviously increases (curve b). However, the currents decrease after assembling of BSA (curve c) due to the poor conductivity of biomolecules. After Apt/BSA/AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE is incubated with BPA solution, the currents further decrease (curve d), resulting from the absorbed BPA hindering [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- to the modified GCE [26].

Besides, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to perform the preparation procedure of the aptasensor (Fig. 4B) and the Randles equivalent circuit was selected to fit with the impedance spectra (Inset of Fig. 4B) [39]. The fitting parameters mainly include the resistance of electron transfer resistance (Ret), the electrolyte solution (Rs), Warburg impedance (Zw) and constant phase element (Q). By calculation, the Ret of AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE is 75 Ω (curve a). Upon the modification of thiolated aptamer, Ret decreases to 24 Ω (curve b), which verifies the success of aptamer modification process. However, Ret increases to 130 Ω and 166 Ω with the immobilization of BSA and BPA, respectively (Fig. 4B, curve c, d). These EIS results consist well with those of CV behaviors, proving the successful construction of the ECL aptasensor.

Next, we investigated the ECL performance of the aptasensor (Fig. 4C). After electrodeposition of AuPs, the ECL signal reaches to 8795 a.u. (curve a). When the thiol-modified aptamer is assembled on AuPs surface, the ECL intensity increases by 2567 a.u. (curve b). After that, a nontargeting protein (1% BSA) is incubated with the as-prepared electrode to block the non-specific sites, resulting in a decreased ECL signal (curve c) owing to its poor conductivity of biomolecules. Finally, when the BSA/Apt/AuPs/NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE is incubated in BPA (10−5 mol L−1), the ECL signal further reduces to 2530 a. u. (curve d). These findings demonstrate that each step of ECL aptasensor is successfully constructed.

Subsequently, we further studied the superiority of the constructed aptasensor. The experiments without aptamer, with aptamer and with random DNA sequence of the same length as aptamer were compared by contrasting the ECL signal. Without aptamer, the ECL signal of the sensor decreases by 26% after incubation with 10−5 mol L−1 BPA (Fig. 4D), since the energy will transfer from exited-state Ru(bpy)3 2+ to oxidation product of BPA [24,40,41]. Interestingly, with aptamer, the ECL signal loses 53% after incubation with 10−5 mol L−1 BPA (Fig. 4D). With random DNA sequence of the same length as BPA aptamer, the ECL signal loses 24% after incubation with 10−5 mol L−1 BPA (Fig. 4D). In comprision, the decreased signal value of the prepared aptasensor is highest, illustrating that the introduction of aptamer improves signal quenching efficiency, which should be attributed to the fact that aptamer can adsorb more BPA molecules onto the electrode surface [26]. Therefore, using the constructed aptasensor to detect BPA is expected to obtain higher sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimizatLD, fluorescence detection; GCE/AuNPs/rGO-Fc-NH2, AuNPs/reduced graphene oxidion of experimental parameters
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the analytical properties for BPA detection, several experimental conditions, including the electrodeposition time and potential of AuPs, the concentration of aptamer, incubation time between aptamer and AuPs, binding time of BPA with aptamer and pH value of buffer solution were optimized in detail. Effect of the electrodeposition time is shown in Fig. 5 A. With the electrodeposition time increases, the ECL signal gradually increases from 5 to 15 s, and begins to decrease after 15 s. Similarly, the strongest ECL intensity is obtained when the electrodeposition potential is - 0.2 V (Fig. 5B). Therefore, the electrodeposition conditions are - 0.2 V for 15 s.

The aptamer concentration and incubation time were optimized. In Fig. 5C, the ECL increases obviously as aptamer concentration rises from 5.0 × 10−7 to 2.0 × 10−6 mol L−1, indicating that more aptamers are assembled on the electrode surface. After higher concentration, it begins to decrease. As shown in Fig. 5D, the behavior of the ECL aptasensor is measured by varying the incubation time between aptamer and AuPs from 12 to 18 h, and the highest ECL signal is obtained at 14 h. Therefore, 2.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 and 14 h are chosen for further studies.

The binding time of BPA with aptamer was also investigated. The prepared aptasensors were incubated with 10−5 mol L−1 BPA for different time intervals. As exhibited in Fig. 5E, the ECL signal decreases significantly with raising the binding time from 10 to 40 min and inclines to a steady platform after 40 min, illustrating the formation of saturated BPA-aptamer complex. Hence, 40 min works as the binding time.

pH value of buffer solution was an important parameter that influenced the structure and performance of aptamer [42]. In Fig. 5F, the ECL response of the modified electrode increases with the pH value increasing from 6.0 to 7.5, and decreases with which changing from 7.5 to 8.5. The reason can be that the structure and property of aptamer are destroyed by the too low or too high pH value, and the affinity between AuPs and aptamer are thereby further decreased [42]. Consequently, PBS at pH = 7.5 is chosen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performances of the BPA ECL aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Analytical properties of the novel ECL aptasensor for BPA were investigated. The higher the concentration of BPA is, the lower the ECL signal is (Fig. 6 A and B). It was attributed to the selective identification of BPA to its aptamer. The ECL response is proportional to the BPA concentration (logarithm) in the range (1.0 × 10−10 - 1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1) with a relatively low detection limit of 3.3 × 10−11 mol L−1. The linear regression equation is IECL = - 558.9–599.4 logCBPA (R2 = 0.9904). The analytical properties of the ECL aptasensor were compared with the previous methods. As we can clearly see from Table 1 , the developed ECL aptasensor exhibits better detection performances such as wider liner range and lower detection limit than those of traditional chromatographic approaches [9,11,43], especially than GC-MS and CE-UV methods. More importantly, the ECL aptasensor is easier and time-saving to operate. When the performances are compared with EC or ECL sensors without aptamer [12,17,24], the linear range and detection limit are also more competitive. Moreover, the fabricated ECL aptasensor has superiority in selective aspect due to the introduction of aptamer. Finally, compared with other aptasensors based on EC [26] and photoelectrochemical (PEC) [28], the present ECL aptasensor shows the best detection capability. Compared with the similar ECL aptasensor based on upconversion nanoparticles [29], wide linear range and low detection limit are obtained by our ECL aptasensor. In addition, compared with Ru(phen)3 2+-TPA system-based ECL aptasenor [30], the prepared RuNSs-NCDs@PEI-based aptasensor selects NCDs@PEI as co-reactant to replace traditional TPA, and exhibits wide linear range. The above results indicate potential application of this ECL aptasenors for specific analysis of BPA.

To confirm the selectivity of the as-prepared ECL aptasensor, the ECL responses toward some potential interference agents with 50-fold concentration with and without BPA aptamer were investigated in detail. In contrast, when there is no aptamer, BPA, 4′-dihydroxydiphenyl, hydroquinone and diethylstilbestrol lead the ECL intensity to decrease by 26%, 30%, 38% and 92%, respectively (Fig. 6C). As can be seen, the changed ECL value caused by interference agents cannot be ignored. These interfering agents have a similar structure to BPA. And they all form benzoquinone derivatives on the GCE surface, then energy transfers from the excited-state Ru(bpy)3 2+ to these benzoquinone derivatives, resulting in signal reduction [41]. Satisfactorily, with the presence of BPA aptamer (Figs. 6D), 1.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 BPA can lead the ECL intensity to decrease by 54%, while the signal changes of 50-fold interferers concentration are within 15%. The results illustrate the high selectivity for BPA determination.

The stability and repeatability of the ECL aptasensor were investigated in detail. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the prepared aptasensor for 12 successive measurements is 1.3% (Fig. 6E), showing ideal stability of the ECL aptasensor. In addition, repeatability for BPA detection by 5 parallel measurements is recorded and the RSD is 1.8% (Fig. 6F), exhibiting a satisfactory repeatability.

The practical detection of BPA was respectively performed in water samples by standard addition method. The recoveries were in the range of 95.5–111.2% with the RSD of 2.5–5.5% (Table 2 ). To verify the reliability of the ECL aptasensor, HPLC-UV method was conducted, and its recoveries were in the range of 85.2–107.0%. As expected, the analytical data of the ECL aptasensor are well consistent with those obtained by HPLC-UV method, demonstrating the good accuracy and reliability of the aptasensor in real samples.

In summary, based on the good advantages of RuNSs-NCDs@PEI ECL system, we firstly developed an ECL aptasensor for the specific determination of BPA. Herein, NCDs@PEI was worked as efficient co-reactant of RuNSs with larger surface-to-volume ratio. BPA aptamer was introduced to improve the selectivity. The as-developed ECL aptasensor exhibited a satisfactory analytical performance, especially good stability and repeatability. More importantly, the constructed ECL aptasensor was of higher specificity than that without aptamer. The as-developed ECL aptasensor was successfully applied to determinate BPA. The recovery results agreed well with those of HPLC-UV method, proving good accuracy and reliability of the ECL aptasensor. Satisfactorily, this ECL aptasensor was a new strategy for BPA in water samples.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675065, 61801195), the “333 Project” (BRA2017390) and the Six Categories Talent Peak of Jiangsu Province (NY-011), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M632238), the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (2014–37).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2019.07.035.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensors and biosensors for analysis of bisphenol A
